pages.full_text:
went ahead & picked a few berries, respearing them on thorns lower down the bush, for John to pick his own, whereupon John's slow plod changed into a jog.
	Postman Pat country – we even saw a van. Many bridleways for walkers.
	Walls in Northumberland
often have a double row of sticking-out stones :–

[sketch of Northumberland wall with double row of protruding stones]

Whereas the ones here in North Yorkshire have built-in stiles, and under-holes for sheep, with tall-ladders for hikers.
[inserted above: longstones right through]

[sketch of North Yorkshire wall with stile, under-hole, and tall ladder]

Straight after lunch we went to Mr. Kirk's riding pony trekking, for the boys, but he didn't have any space. He's a cousin of the campsite
pages.notes: Page begins mid-sentence, continuing from previous spread. Contains two hand-drawn sketches illustrating dry-stone wall construction: one showing Northumberland style (double row of protruding stones) and one showing North Yorkshire style (built-in stiles, under-holes for sheep, tall ladders for hikers). 'respearing' may be 'respacing' or another word — transcribed as written. 'Mr. Kirk' is a person name, not a place. Sentence continues onto right page.

pages.full_text: farmer. So we went shopping in Settle instead, looking for places to sell

We found an interesting shop – Holland & Daughter. (but she's married now) The owner sells hand knitwear from [out] Aran a rare breed of sheep. She might sell our Arans. She rowed with a woman on the telephone who had sent her a

We bought delicious cakes & pies at the bakery.
	The farm shop on the campsite sells lovely fresh milk, Wensleydale cheese, eggs & cream & orange juice. Loo clean.
	The farmer & his wife were very nice. He was very friendly & patient explaining about the milking. We saw the cows being brought down for milking & watched the milking in the milking parlour – 6 at a time.
	Thomas & William sent out to sleep in the tent. Much fighting among Thomas, William & Peter.
Sat We are stopping in at Burnley to see
pages.notes: Page number in top-right corner appears to be 11, partially visible. Page begins mid-sentence continuing from left page ('farmer.'). 'Decorum' appears to be a product name (knitwear brand) in quotes. The shop name reads 'Holland & Daughter'. 'Aran' refers to Aran wool/sheep. The word 'out' with a strikethrough before 'Aran' suggests a correction. Sentence at the bottom ends mid-page, continuing to next spread. 'the Lakes' likely refers to the Lake District. Year cannot be confirmed from this page alone; volume covers 1989–2003. Page number 10/11 suggests early in the diary, consistent with an early trip in the volume.
